coredata.dc:title: Fast one-pot microwave-assisted green synthesis of highly fluorescent plant-inspired S,N-self-doped carbon quantum dots as a sensitive probe for the antiviral drug nitazoxanide and hemoglobin
coredata.dc:creator: Qandeel, Nermeen A.
El-Masry, Amal A.
Eid, Manal
Moustafa, Mohamed A.
El-Shaheny, Rania
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we report a one-pot, green, cost-efficient, and fast synthesis of plant-based sulfur and nitrogen self-co-doped carbon quantum dots (S,N-CQDs). By 4-min microwave treatment of onion and cabbage juices as renewable, cheap, and green carbon sources and self-passivation agents, blue emissive S,N-CQDs have been synthesized (λex/λem of 340/418 nm) with a fluorescence quantum yield of 15.2%. A full characterization of the natural biomass-derived quantum dots proved the self-doping with nitrogen and sulfur. The S,N-CQDs showed high efficiency as a fluorescence probe for sensitive determination of nitazoxanide (NTZ), that recently found wide applicability as a repurposed drug for COVID-19, over the concentration range of 0.25–50.0 μM with LOD of 0.07 μM. The nanoprobe has been successfully applied for NTZ determination in pharmaceutical samples with excellent % recovery of 98.14 ± 0.42. Furthermore, the S,N-CQDs proved excellent performance as a sensitive fluorescence nanoprobe for determination of hemoglobin (Hb) over the concentration range of 36.3–907.5 nM with a minimum detectability of 10.30 nM. The probe has been applied for the determination of Hb in blood samples showing excellent agreement with the results documented by a medical laboratory. The greenness of the developed probe has been positively investigated by different greenness metrics and software. The green character of the proposed analytical methods originates from the synthesis of S,N-CQDs from sustainable, widely available, and cheap plants via low energy/low cost microwave-assisted technique. Omission of organic solvents and harsh chemicals beside dependence on mix-and-read analytical approach corroborate the method greenness. The obtained results demonstrated the substantial potential of the synthesized green, safe, cheap, and sustainable S,N-CQDs for pharmaceutical and biological applications.

head.ce:title: Fast one-pot microwave-assisted green synthesis of highly fluorescent plant-inspired S,N-self-doped carbon quantum dots as a sensitive probe for the antiviral drug nitazoxanide and hemoglobin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Medicinal Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura, 35516, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Medicinal Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura, 35516, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura, 35516, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura, 35516, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we report a one-pot, green, cost-efficient, and fast synthesis of plant-based sulfur and nitrogen self-co-doped carbon quantum dots (S,N-CQDs). By 4-min microwave treatment of onion and cabbage juices as renewable, cheap, and green carbon sources and self-passivation agents, blue emissive S,N-CQDs have been synthesized (λex/λem of 340/418 nm) with a fluorescence quantum yield of 15.2%. A full characterization of the natural biomass-derived quantum dots proved the self-doping with nitrogen and sulfur. The S,N-CQDs showed high efficiency as a fluorescence probe for sensitive determination of nitazoxanide (NTZ), that recently found wide applicability as a repurposed drug for COVID-19, over the concentration range of 0.25–50.0 μM with LOD of 0.07 μM. The nanoprobe has been successfully applied for NTZ determination in pharmaceutical samples with excellent % recovery of 98.14 ± 0.42. Furthermore, the S,N-CQDs proved excellent performance as a sensitive fluorescence nanoprobe for determination of hemoglobin (Hb) over the concentration range of 36.3–907.5 nM with a minimum detectability of 10.30 nM. The probe has been applied for the determination of Hb in blood samples showing excellent agreement with the results documented by a medical laboratory. The greenness of the developed probe has been positively investigated by different greenness metrics and software. The green character of the proposed analytical methods originates from the synthesis of S,N-CQDs from sustainable, widely available, and cheap plants via low energy/low cost microwave-assisted technique. Omission of organic solvents and harsh chemicals beside dependence on mix-and-read analytical approach corroborate the method greenness. The obtained results demonstrated the substantial potential of the synthesized green, safe, cheap, and sustainable S,N-CQDs for pharmaceutical and biological applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last decade, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been established as an efficient analytical sensor owing to their distinctive optical characteristics, excellent water solubility, biocompatibility, non-toxicity, and ease of functionalization. CQDs are now positioned as a superb alternative to fluorescent dyes and fluorescence derivatizing agents due to their exceptional and tailorable fluorescence properties. As well, they are more eco-friendly and less toxic than metal-based nanomaterial [1].

Two main approaches are currently employed for the synthesis of CQDs: (i) top-down approach by processing graphite through multiple steps and strong chemicals to form graphite oxide that is crashed into nano-sized quantum dots, and (ii) down-top approach by polymerization and carbonization of small molecules into CQDs. The later approach is the more widely applied one since it is simpler and easier. The main techniques for synthesis of CQDs via the down-top approach include hydrothermal, solvothermal, and dry heating. However, these techniques entail many disadvantages that contradict the concept of green chemistry since they require very long time that may exceed 24 h, very high temperature up to 300 °C, and utilize some harsh chemicals and organic solvents [1,2]. Yet, the global claims to incorporating green chemistry and safety into laboratory culture mandate developing new green methods for fabrication of reliable, sustainable, and eco-friendly CQDs [1].

Thus, in this study we designed an integrated approach for greening both the synthesis procedure and the carbon source to reconquest the greenness and sustainability of the synthesis of CQDs. The devised approach is based on using low-energy microwave-assisted synthesis at 700 W for 4 min only. Furthermore, we managed to use cheap, abundant, and easily accessible plants, cabbage (Brassica oleracea var. capitata) and onion (Allium cepa L.), as renewable carbon feedstocks. Indeed, cabbage and onion are much cheaper than other reported biomaterials for CQDs synthesis. Furthermore, onion is rich in sulfur-containing components [3] while cabbage, as a leafy green, is a known source of nitrogen and protein. These facts suggest the possibility of self-doping of the CQDs with nitrogen and sulfur by combining these two natural biomasses as carbon sources and self-passivation agents. The as-prepared S,N-self-doped CQDs (S,N-CQDs) showed a strong fluorescence, excellent water-solubility, and very good stability that enabled their application as a highly efficient fluorescence sensor for pharmaceutical and biological analysis. Thus, in this contribution, we successfully applied the prepared S,N-CQDs for quantitative determination of the antiprotozoal/antiparasitic/antiviral drug nitazoxanide (NTZ) in pharmaceutical formulation. Additionally, the prepared S,N-CQDs has been applied for quantitative determination of hemoglobin (Hb) in blood.

NTZ (N-(5-nitro-2-thiazolyl) salicylamide acetate) is nitrothiazole antiprotozoal and antiparasitic compound used for the treatment of cryptosporidiosis, giardiasis, and other protozoal and helminth infections especially in immunocompetent patients [4]. Furthermore, NTZ has a broad-spectrum antiviral activity against several viral infections such as influenza, coronaviruses, hepatitis C virus, and hepatitis B virus. It is worth noting that drug repurposing has also drawn great interest in NTZ as a new therapy for cancer, severe asthma, and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease that is poorly controlled with existing therapies [5]. During the current pandemic of COVID-19, drug repurposing policy becomes a practical approach to find new clinical uses for present medicines. In this context, repurposing of NTZ as an efficient therapy for COVID-19 has been clinically proven. NTZ has good antiviral activity against SARS-COV-2, and it enhances the host immune response thus combats cytokines storm and recovers multiple organ damage [6–9]. Administration of NTZ may be an effective initial intervention against SARS-COV-2 to prevent complications, lessening the institutional and social viral spread and thus, death rate [10]. Hence, NTZ provides a great potential and value addition to the patients with COVID-19 [6–9].

So far, the analytical methods reported for the determination of NTZ include HPLC [11–14], thin layer chromatography [15], spectrophotometry [15,16], and electrochemical methods [17]. These methods either lack the sufficient sensitivity, need expensive apparatus or experienced operator, or utilize harmful chemicals or organic solvents, which significantly impede their wide applications. In comparison with these methods, fluorometric analysis is simpler, more sensitive, less expensive, and easily accomplished in a shorter time. Yet, no fluorometric assay has been reported for the determination of NTZ [18]. This prompted us to utilize the developed CQDs for highly sensitive sensing of NTZ as the first fluorescence-based analysis, where the intense fluorescence of S,N-CQDs is gradually quenched with increasing NTZ concentration via inner filter effect mechanism.

Additionally, the S,N-CQDs has been successfully applied for quantitative determination of Hb. Hb is a metalloprotein in the red blood cells (RBCs) responsible for carrying oxygen. Low levels of Hb may be a symptom of numerous clinical conditions including different types of anemia, blood disorders, heart diseases [19], kidney diseases [20], liver diseases [21], and cancer [22]. The WHO defines the normal levels of Hb as 12 g/dL for women and 13 g/dL for men, and Hb levels below these are considered anemic [23]. Accurate quantitation of Hb is a basic test for clinical diagnostic to evaluate anemia and other diseases. Thus, it is necessary that procedures for Hb determination are sensitive, specific, accurate, and reproducible. Despite there are many documented methods for Hb determination [24], these methods are not sufficiently sensitive to detect low concentrations of Hb which is very important for clinical diagnostics of some health conditions such as hemolysis and hematuria. From this perspective, the prepared S,N-CQDs have been applied for trace determination of Hb with high simplicity, sensitivity, precision, and accuracy.

Fluorescence measurements were done using Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, USA) fitted with a Xenon flash lamp and operated at 800 V with a scan speed of 600 nm/min and 5 nm slit width for excitation and emission monochromators. UV-VIS absorption spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu Spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan). A centrifuge (model 2-16P, Germany), Eumax ultrasonic bath (model SS 101H 230, USA) and thermostatically controlled water bath (Cambridge Ltd, UK) were used. A consort NV P‐901 pH meter (Belgium) was used for pH measurements. A Smart SM-2316MH microwave (China) has been used for the synthesis of CQDs.

All chemicals and solvents used were of analytical grade. Boric acid, sodium hydroxide, calcium chloride, sodium chloride, magnesium sulfate, potassium chloride, glucose, sucrose, citric acid, sodium benzoate, sodium citrate, and quinine sulfate were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., U.S.A. Hydrogen peroxide, sodium acetate, and acetic acid were obtained from Adwic Co. (Cairo, Egypt). Paracetamol was kindly donated by EIPICO Co. (10th of Ramadan City, Egypt), spiramycin was kindly gifted by Delta Pharma S.A.E (10th of Ramadan City, Egypt), ribavirin, diclofenac, and human lyophilized hemoglobin were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., U.S.A. Parazoxanide capsules labelled to contain 500 mg NTZ/capsule, product of Pharma Care Co, Cairo, Egypt, were purchased from a local Pharmacy.

Britton Robinson buffer (BRB) was prepared by adjusting the pH of aqueous solution containing 0.04 M boric acid, 0.04 M orthophosphoric acid, and 0.04 M acetic acid with 0.2 M sodium hydroxide. 0.02 M acetate buffer of pH 5.0 was prepared by adjusting the pH of 0.02 M sodium acetate with acetic acid. A stock solution of NTZ (0.5 mM) was prepared in ethanol and diluted with the same solvent to prepare a standard NTZ solution (0.05 mM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Eco-friendly synthesis of S, N co-doped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh cabbage and onions were bought from a local supermarket in Mansoura, Egypt. The leaves of the cabbage were separated, washed thoroughly with tap water followed by distilled water, and sliced into small pieces. The pieces were finely grounded in a domestic fruit mixer. The obtained mixture was passed through a muslin cloth to obtain pulp-free juice. Similarly, the outer layers of onion were removed, and it was rinsed with tap water followed by distilled water then chopped into small pieces. The chopped onion was processed by the fruit mixer followed by filtration with a muslin cloth to get pulp-free onion juice. The obtained juices were well mixed in a ratio of (10:10, v/v) in a small beaker then placed in a domestic microwave at 700 W for 4 min. After cooling to room temperature, the resultant residue was dispersed in 20 mL distilled water and sonicated for 30 min. Then, the mixture was filtered and centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 20 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.45 μm syringe filter. The obtained dark-brown solution was stored in amber-colored bottle in the refrigerator at 4 °C for future use, it was stable up to 6 months under this condition.

The morphology and size of the synthesized CQDs have been investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging using a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) working at 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was applied to investigate the chemical structure of the synthesized CQDs using Thermo-Fisher Scientific Nicolet—iS10 FT-IR spectrometer. Zeta potential of the CQDs was measured using Malvern Zetasizer (Malvern Panalytical, Ltd, Worcestershire, UK). Optical properties including absorbance and fluorescence were also investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of fluorescence quantum yield of the S,N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum yield (Ф) of the prepared S,N-CQDs was calculated using quinine sulfate as a reference (prepared in 0.1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution, fluorescence Ф of 54%) [25]. Standard solutions of quinine sulfate and S,N-CQDs with absorbance less than 0.05 at λex, to minimize inner filter effect (IFE), have been prepared. The integrated fluorescence of the solutions has been calculated. The Ф of S,N-CQDs has been calculated using the following equation [25]:
(1)

Where, F is the integrated fluorescence, A is the absorbance, and n is the refractive index of the solvent. The symbol (St) denotes the standard quinine, and the symbol (x) indicates the CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of calibration curve for NTZ
ce:section.ce:para: For the fluorescence measurement, increasing volumes of NTZ standard solution were transferred to a series of 10 mL volumetric flasks followed by 1000 μL of BRB (pH 8.0) and 500 μL of S,N-CQDs. The volumes were completed with distilled water and the solutions were well-mixed. The fluorescence (F) was measured at 418 nm after excitation at 340 nm. A blank experiment was done omitting the drug and its fluorescence (F0) was measured. Each experiment has been performed in triplicate. The calibration curve has been constructed by plotting Ln(F0/F) versus NTZ concentration [μM] and the regression equation has been derived.

The contents of ten capsules were carefully emptied and accurately weighed. An amount of the powder has been weighed and transferred to 100 mL volumetric flask to prepare 0.5 mM solution of NTZ. The final volume of the flask was completed with ethanol and dissolved with the aid of sonication. The mixture was filtered, and different volumes of the filtrate have been transferred to a series of 10 mL volumetric flasks then the procedure described in Sections “2.6.1.” was followed to calculate the concentrations of NTZ. The % recovery and standard deviation were calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of calibration curve for Hb
ce:section.ce:para: Different volumes of Hb solution were transferred into a group of 10 mL volumetric flasks to yield final concentrations of 36.3–907.5 nM and mixed with 500 μL of S,N-CQDs. The volume was made up to 10 mL with acetate buffer pH 5.0. The solutions were thoroughly mixed, and the fluorescence (F) was measured at 340/418 nm. A blank experiment was performed in parallel omitting Hb and its fluorescence was measured (F0). Each experiment was carried out in triplicate and the average values of Ln(F0/F)were calculated and plotted versus Hb concentration [nM].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Hb in blood samples
ce:section.ce:para: Blood samples were obtained from a medical analysis laboratory in Mansoura City, Egypt to compare the results obtained by the laboratory with that of the proposed method. For determination of Hb by the S,N-CQDs, the method reported by Lu et al. [26] has been used with some modifications: 100 μL from each blood sample was diluted with pure water to 10 mL and incubated for 20 min to permit the release of Hb from RBCs. The solution was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min, aliquots of the supernatant were added to 500 μL of S,N-CQDs, the volumes were made up to 10 mL with acetate buffer pH 5.0. The solutions were thoroughly mixed, and the fluorescence was measured at 340/418 nm. The concentration of Hb in each sample was calculated using the regression equation.

The developed strategy for microwave-assisted synthesis of S,N-CQDs enabled achieving the complete synthesis process in few minutes using low energy of 700 W in a regular household microwave. Furthermore, the use of plants as a cheap, green, abundant, and renewable feedstock is a key merit of the synthesis procedure. The combination of these two tactics offered a prominent greenness feature to the developed procedure. Besides, onion and cabbage are popular vegetables, widely available, and easily accessible all over the world. This supports the wide applicability of the designed method.

Initially, the influence of the ratio of onion: cabbage juices has been studied (0:10, 5:15, 10:10, 15:5, and 10:0, v/v, respectively) while fixing the microwave irradiation time and the final volume of the starting solution at 20 mL. The highest quantum yield was noticed at 340/418 nm (excitation/emission) using onion and cabbage juices in the ratio of 10:10 (v/v) (Fig. 1 a). Additionally, this volumetric ratio has been used to study the microwave irradiation time, keeping the same final volume. As can be deduced from Fig. 1b, 4 min is the optimum microwave irradiation time for CQDs synthesis as revealed from the highest quantum yield. This synthesis procedure is ultrafast as compared to the reported hydrothermal methods for synthesis of CQDs from biomasses. This is attributed to the ability of microwaves to direct and selective heating through resonance with the vibrational frequencies of molecules, thus the microwave reaction is often complete before a significant alteration in temperature of a corresponding hydrothermal synthesis is reached [1]. Fig. 2 depicts schematic illustration of the steps of synthesis of CQDs by the devised method.

To demonstrate the reproducibility and robustness of the developed synthesis procedure, different varieties of onion and cabbage have been tried as starting substrates. Red and yellow onions, as well as green and white cabbages were examined. As well, plants collected in different seasons: winter (December and February), autumn (September), and spring (March and April) were also investigated. Besides, plants obtained from different Egyptian regions were utilized for the synthesis process. Results of this study are illustrated in Fig. 3
. In all investigated alternates, no change in the fluorescence wavelengths has been observed. Furthermore, the small values of relative standard deviations (%RSD) among S,N-CQDs independently synthesized from different plant varieties, plants collected in different seasons, and plants collected from different cultivation areas (7.0, 1.9, and 1.8%, respectively) confirm the robustness of the developed synthesis procedure and its validity to be reproduced in most laboratories at different regions with available materials.

TEM imaging was performed to explore the morphological features and size of the CQDs. Fig. 4a shows that the synthesized CQDs are sphere-shaped with average size of 12.6 ± 3.16 nm. The surface structure and functional groups of the synthesized CQDs has been investigated by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 4b). The strong broad peak at 3333 cm−1 is attributed to stretching vibration of O–H or N–H, and the peak at 2784 cm−1 is assigned to C–H stretching vibration. The strong narrow peak at 1641 cm−1 is attributed to C=C, C=O, or C=N stretching vibration. The peaks at 1063 and 1030 cm−1 are assigned to S=O stretching while the peak at 921 cm−1 is attributed to O–H bending or S–N stretching. Besides, the peak at 669 cm−1 referred to C–S or C–S–C stretching vibration. The peaks at 602, 560, 534 cm−1 ascribed S–S stretching vibration [27]. The FTIR spectra prove the successful inclusion of copious nitrogen, oxygen, and sulfur-containing functional groups on the surfaces of CQDs, granting high polarity and water solubility. This result has been confirmed by zeta potential (ζ) measurement (Fig. 4c). The surface charge of the S,N-CQDs was estimated by determination of its ζ, that was found to equals −24.2 mv. The high negative value of ζ provides stability to the S,N-CQDs since the repulsion between the negatively charged CQDs prevents their aggregation. The negative value of ζ is attributed to the surface functional groups.

The self-doped oxygen, sulfur, and nitrogen-containing CQDs likely result from successive dehydration, polymerization, carbonization, and surface-passivation in the presence of carbonaceous organic components, such as carbohydrates, sugars, fatty acids, proteins, and amino acids in cabbage [28], and phenolics, proteins, polysaccharides, and numerous sulfurous compounds e.g. thiosulfinates, thiosulfonates, sulfides, sulfoxides, and S-oxides [29] in onion, during the microwave treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and stability of the S,N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption spectrum of S,N-CQDs shows two well resolved bands at 210 and 290 nm as depicted in Fig. 5 a. The far UV-band at 210 nm is assigned to π-π* transition of the CC of the aromatic core of the CQDs. While the near UV-band around 290 nm is assigned to n–π* transition of carbonyl/amine functional groups at the surface of CQDs [30].

The S,N-CQDs shows an intense blue fluorescence at 418 nm upon excitation at 340 nm (Fig. 5a). The S,N-CQDs exhibited excitation-dependent emission as can be noted from Fig. 5b. Changing the excitation wavelength from 300 to 340 did not cause a significant shift of the maximum emission wavelength while the FI increases stepwise up to 340 nm. Further increase of the excitation wavelength from 350 to 400 nm leads to excitation dependent down-conversion fluorescence emission associated with gradual decrease of FI. This excitation-dependent behavior is similar to that reported in the literature and attributed to the surface states that affect the band gap [31]. The highest FI was achieved at λex/λem of 340/418 nm. The quantum yield of the S,N-CQDs was estimated to be 15.2% using quinine sulfate as a reference, proving good fluorescence properties as compared to CQDs prepared from different biomasses [32–43].

The stability of fluorescent materials is a fundamental factor for using CQDs as a fluorescence probe. The effect of pH on the FI of the S,N-CQDs has been investigated over the pH range of 2.0–12.0 using BRB. As demonstrated in Fig. 5c, a decrease of FI is observed only if the pH was less than 3 or higher than 8. It was notable that the FI reduced by about 20% only under extreme pH conditions. This pH-responsive behavior is well-reported in the literature and interpreted by several mechanisms such as protonation/deprotonation of surface function groups and electron transfer between function groups and CQDs [44]. Thus, for the present S,N-CQDs, it is expected that the surface function groups begin to be protonated at strongly acidic pH (˂ pH 3.0) which leads to non-covalent molecular interaction, aggregation of the CQDs, and decrease of their fluorescence [45]. Furthermore, at this acidic pH, protonation of the surface amino groups enhances the intramolecular electron transfer ability between –NH3 + and CQDs, giving rise to fluorescence quenching [46]. While over the pH range of 3.0–8.0, the surface oxygen-containing groups become deprotonated (-COO- and -O-). That leads to the increase of surface negative charges and prevents CQDs aggregation, thus, enhances their fluorescence. On the other hand, at strongly alkaline pH (pH ˃ 8), fluorescence quenching is likely to take place via electron transfer process from the electron lone pair of surface amino group to the CQDs [47].

The high and consistent FI over the physiological pH range is an advantage for the synthesized S,N-CQDs. Furthermore, the susceptibility of the S,N-CQDs to photobleaching has been investigated by subjecting S,N-CQDs solution to continuous UV-radiation at 365 nm. Only 4% drop of fluorescence intensity was observed after 30 min. Besides, the S,N-CQDs solution shows excellent stability with time for more than 6 months without any sign of precipitation, floatation, or turbidity, showing a stable quantum yield (quantum yield changed by about 1.7% after 6 months of storage at 4 °C). These results confirm the high stability of the synthesized S,N-CQDs and its potential for various practical applications.

In conclusion, the existence of abundant surface function groups, high quantum yield, and excellent stability show prospective sensing properties of the synthesized S,N-CQDs. It is evident that, the proposed microwave synthesis procedure is advantageous as compared to reported synthesis methods owing to greening of both the synthesis technique and the carbon source. The low cost, simple implementation, very fast synthesis (120–720 times faster than hydrothermal [32–36,43] and 45 times faster than dry heat [42] methods) highlight the high efficiency of the synthesis technique (Table 1 ). It can be noted from Table 1 that the fluorescence quantum yield of the synthesized S,N-CQDs is better than those of the reported systems [32–43]. The present system has significant merits of being derived from cheap and widely available plants, complete omission of chemicals and organic solvents, and consumption of minimum energy. This imparts excellent and unique green character to the new probe.

Fig. 6 a demonstrated that the strong fluorescence of S,N-CQDs at 418 nm is steadily reduced with the addition of increasing concentrations of NTZ (0.25–50.0 μM). According to the obtained results, about 80% of S,N-CQDs fluorescence was quenched in presence of 50.0 μM NTZ. As a start point to explore the mechanism involved in the observed S,N-CQDs fluorescence quenching in presence of NTZ, the possible incorporation of inner filter effect (IFE) has been investigated. The absorption spectrum of NTZ at pH 8.0 exhibits an absorption maximum at 416 nm showing a complete overlap with the emission spectrum of S,N-CQDs, in addition to a significant absorption at 340 nm (Fig. 5a), indicating the possibility of primary and secondary IFE that is generated when the quencher absorbs the excitation and emission wavelengths of the fluorophore, respectively [48]. To further confirm the suggested mechanism, correction for IFE has been carried out by calculation of the corrected FI after removal of IFE (Fcorrected) using Eq. (2) [48]:
(2)

Where; Fcorrected is the corrected FI after removal of IFE, Fobserved is the observed FI, and Aex and Aem are the absorbance of NTZ at the excitation and emission wavelengths of the S,N-CQDs, respectively.

This step was followed by calculation of the % suppressed efficiency (%E) for the corrected and observed FI according to Eq. (3) [48]:
(3)
% E =

The results illustrated in Fig. 6b confirmed that IFE is the main trigger of quenching of S,N-CQDs fluorescence by NTZ since a great loss of suppression efficiency takes place by subtraction of IFE.

A further evidence of the quenching mechanism has been demonstrated by Stern-Volmer analysis of the fluorescence quenching that has been conducted at increasing temperature settings (310, 320, 330 K) according to Eq. (4) [49].
(4)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
s v
[
N T Z
]
Where Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and [NTZ] is the molar concentration of NTZ. Dynamic quenching takes place due to return of the excited state fluorophore to the ground state by collision with the quencher, while static quenching happens due to formation of non-fluorescent ground state complex of fluorophore-quencher. Ksv increases with increasing the temperature in case of dynamic quenching while it decreases with increasing the temperature in case of static quenching. On the other hand, the process of IFE is neither static nor dynamic quenching, and it is not dependent on temperature [49]. Plots of
F 0
F
versus [NTZ] at different temperature settings are shown in Fig. 6c. The data in Table 2 revealed that the Ksv shows very small variation with temperature, and it is not temperature dependent. Thus, both static and dynamic quenching are excluded, and the possible quenching mechanism is IFE.

It is worth mentioning herein that the concentration-dependent red shift (CDRS) of fluorescence peak maxima of S,N-CQDs in presence of increasing concentrations of NTZ (Fig. 6a) is a characteristic of systems involving IFE and it is so-called IFE-induced CDRS. The λmax of fluorescence gets red-shifted at high concentrations of NTZ due to higher IFE at such high concentrations. This phenomenon is well-known and authenticated in systems involving secondary IFE, takes place due to overlap of quencher's absorption spectrum with fluorophore's emission spectrum, due to absorption of radiation from the blue side of the fluorescence emission maxima [50–53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of analytical conditions for NTZ determination
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the optimal analytical conditions and promote the sensitive determination of NTZ, the influences of different experimental conditions including pH, buffer volume, and S,N-CQDs amounts on F0/F were studied. First, the influence of pH has been investigated using BRB of pH ranges from 2.0 to 12.0. As demonstrated in Fig. 7 a, the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) was low in acidic pH (2.0–5.0) and it increased by moving towards more alkaline pH up to pH 8.0 then a plateau region is attained. This behavior is attributed to spectral shift of NTZ absorption spectrum to 420 nm with hyperchromic shift in alkaline pH [16] causing complete overlap with S,N-CQDs emission spectrum (Fig. 5a) and fluorescence quenching by IFE. Thus, pH 8.0 has been selected as the optimum pH for the method. The influence of volume of the buffer has been examined by varying it over the range of 500–2000 μL. As can be seen in Fig. 7b, the volume of buffer did not have a substantial effect on the quenching efficiency. 1000 μL has been selected as the optimum volume of BRB of pH 8.0.

The influence of the amount of S,N-CQDs on the method performance has been also studied (100–700 μL). The best quenching efficiency was obtained using 500 μL (Fig. 7c), thus it was selected as the optimum volume.

To evaluate the detection performance, the fluorescence spectra of the S,N-CQDs were recorded in the presence of various concentrations of NTZ. The values of Ln (F0/F) [53] proved excellent linear relationship with NTZ concentration over the range of 0.25–50.0 μM that fitted to the following regression equation:
(5)
Ln
(
F 0
/ F )
= 0.028 x − 0.0002
( r = 0.9999 )
Where x is the concentration of NTZ [μM] and r is the correlation coefficient. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were calculated as 3.3σ/S, and 10σ/S, respectively, where σ is the standard deviation of the response and S is the slope of the calibration curve. LOD and LOQ equal 0.07 and 0.24 μM, respectively. These results reflect the high sensitivity of the probe.

Besides, the accuracy and precision of the developed method have been evaluated on inter- and intra-day levels. Repeated determination of three concentrations of NTZ for three consecutive times in the same day has been executed to evaluate the inter-day precision of the method, besides, three concentrations of NTZ have been determined for three straight days. The calculated values of the standard deviation (SD) and relative standard deviations (RSD) are indicatives of the high precision of the developed method (Table 3 ).

The results of determination of NTZ by the developed probe have been compared with the results of a reported method [16] by Student t-test and Variance ratio F-test [54]. As outcome from Table 4 , there is no significant difference between the performance of the two methods since the calculated t- and F-values are less than the theoretical values [54].
It is evident from these results that the new probe satisfies all validation criteria set by the ICH guidelines [55].

To demonstrate whether the S,N-CQDs can be used as a bio-probe for determination of Hb, the change of fluorescence of S,N-CQDs upon addition of various concentrations of Hb has been investigated. As can be deduced from Fig. 8 a, the addition of increasing concentrations of Hb causes a stepwise quenching of the fluorescence of S,N-CQDs. The UV-absorption spectrum of Hb overlaps greatly with both the excitation and emission spectra of S,N-CQDs (Fig. 5a), thus it quenched S,N-CQDs fluorescence by IFE [26]. This has been confirmed by correction for IFE [48] as described earlier in section “3.4.”. The observed and corrected fluorescence intensities are figured out in Fig. 8b. It can be noted that 90–98% of the suppressed effect originates from the IFE of Hb.

The IFE-induced CDRS of S,N-CQDs fluorescence spectra is clearly observed in presence of increasing concentrations of Hb (Fig. 8a). As discussed previously, this effect is attributed to the secondary IFE results from the overlap of Hb absorption spectrum with S,N-CQDs emission spectrum that causes absorption of the radiation from the blue side of the fluorescence emission maxima [50–53].

Thus, the quantitative estimation of Hb using the S,N-CQDs has been established. The correlation of Ln (F0/F) and Hb concentration [nM] [53] achieved a linear dynamic range from 36.3 to 907.5 nM (n=7) with a correlation coefficient of 0.9999 and a minimum detectability of 10.3 nM and minimum quantification limit of 31.23 nM [55]. The accuracy of the proposed method is evidenced by the excellent %found and the small values of SD and %RSD as seen in Table 5 .

The detection system revealed excellent intraday precision as evidenced by repeated determination of three Hb concentrations for three successive times within the same day. The calculated % RSD did not exceed 4.36%. Further, the repeatability of the method was examined by determination of three concentrations of Hb on three successive days. The obtained results (%RSD ≤8.35) confirmed the repeatability of the method. The results of precision assessment are abridged in Table 6 . Overall, these results corroborate the applicability of the S,N-CQDs for trace Hb determination.

To evaluate the applicability of the synthesized S,N-CQDs for fluorescence sensing of NTZ and Hb, the effect of different possibly co-existing substances including glycine, glucose, sucrose, citric acid, sodium benzoate, sodium citrate, urea, lysine, nicotinamide, glutathione, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, Ca2+, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Cl−, and SO4 2−, in addition to some drugs from different pharmacological classes comprising paracetamol, diclofenac, spiramycin, and ribavirin, has been explored to determine their impact on the analytical response. The results illustrated in Table 7 confirm that the presence of these substances has no significant influence on the FI of S,N-CQDs, where the % change in the FI ranged from +1.39 to −5.71%, indicating the selectivity of the probe and its reliability for pharmaceutical and biological applications.

The efficacy and feasibility of the designed fluorescence probe has been evaluated for determination of NTZ in commercially marketed capsules. The obtained results are abridged in Table 4. The high %found (97.94–98.62%) and small value of SD (±0.42) ensured the reliability of the developed probe and its suitability for real application without interference from excipients and additives.

The reliability of the S,N-CQDs as a biological probe has been investigated by determination of Hb in three blood samples obtained from a local medical analysis laboratory (Fig. 8c). The results obtained by the proposed method after taking into consideration dilution factor are presented in Table 8
. The perfect agreement of the results obtained by the S,N-CQDs probe with the documented lab method (Table 8) is very promising indicating the excellent performance of the devised probe and its suitability for practical real life application.

The greenness, safety, and economic value of the designed analytical techniques are obvious at first glance by virtue of using low-energy consuming and timely synthesis procedure beside renewable and very safe carbon sources. In this context, the most recent tool and software for assessment of analytical methodology greenness; namely the complementary green analytical procedure index (ComplexGAPI) [56], that is mainly directed to evaluation of analytical methods including the use of materials produced prior to the analytical step, has been applied to assess the proposed method. The result is illustrated in the pictogram shown in Fig. 9 a. The hexagon at the bottom represents the ‘green’ character of the pre-analysis practices. It includes evaluation of the conditions, reagents and solvents, equipment, work up and purification of the end products. The upper pentagonal fields correspond to the green features of the analytical method itself including reagents, procedures, and instrumentations, considering many factors such as amount, health hazard, and safety hazard of reagents and solvents, energy consumption, waste generation, and nature of the method (qualitative/quantitative). The green, yellow, and red are corresponding to low, medium, and high environmental impacts linked to each phase of the pre-analysis process and the analytical method. The obtained pictogram (Fig. 9a) reflects the greenness of the synthesis process of S,N-CQDs as well as the greenness of the analytical method. Besides, the Analytical GREEnness calculator (AGREE) [57] has been also applied to quantitatively estimate the greenness of the proposed method by exploring the quantities and toxicity of reagents, produced waste, energy needs, and the number of practical steps. The overall result of the analysis is given in Fig. 9b showing a final score of 0.84 that indicates the excellent greenness of the method.

The results of the two greenness's metrics show perfect agreement confirming the green character of the proposed technique. The proposed fluorescence probe depends on using CQDs prepared from renewable plant-based sources that are safe to both the environment and human. Furthermore, the synthesis procedure of the CQDs is one-pot, accomplished in a very short time while consuming very much lower amount of energy as compared to the reported hydrothermal methods for synthesis of CQDs. Cost-efficiency is another merit for the developed method since it depends on using cheap and abundant plants as well as regular domestic microwave oven. Minimum use of organic solvents and simplicity of the analytical procedure that depend on mix-and-read approach are other factors enhanced the eco-friendship of the method. All these criteria promote the greenness of the devised analytical technique and support its application for NTZ and Hb determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the S,N-CQDs fluorescence probe with the published methods for the tested analytes
ce:section.ce:para: Comparison of the performance of S,N-CQDs probe for determination of NTZ and its reported analytical literature [11–17] showed many advantages (Table 9). The proposed method is 2–243 times more sensitive than the reported methods for NTZ determination [11–17]. The eco-friendship is another aspect of excellence of the proposed probe. It is noted from that all the reported methods for NTZ consume plenty of organic solvents and harsh chemicals such as acetonitrile, methanol, toluene, chloroform, carbon tetrachloride, mercury, and glacial acetic acid, while the proposed method avoid these hazardous and expensive chemicals. Furthermore, the reported techniques are more time consuming than the proposed method. For example, the HPLC analysis [11–15] requires time for equilibration, conditioning, analysis, and washing.

Similarly, the performance of the S,N-CQDs for quantitative determination of Hb has been compared with its reported analytical methods (Table 10 ). The developed probe is 1.34–67,000 times more sensitive than the reported methods for Hb determination [58–61]. Moreover, the reported methods [58–60] involved high temperature and very lengthy procedures for the synthesis of quantum dots. Additionally, the reported paper-based method [61] involved the use of some hazardous chemicals such as o-toluidine and Drabkin's reagent that contains the very risky and dangerous chemical, potassium cyanide. Despite the reported fluorescence method that depends on WS2 quantum dots [62] is more sensitive than the developed method, it requires multistep hydrothermal synthesis at 200 °C for 8 h using sodium tungstate and glutathione. Thus, the advantages of the developed method over published methods for Hb with respect to rapidness, safety, mild conditions, sustainability, and sensitivity are remarkable. This promotes the proposed method for application for Hb determination and trace detection.

This study presents, for the first time, the utility of cabbage and onion biomasses simply available in local markets for developing straightforward, sensitive, economic, and very rapid method for the synthesis of S,N-CQDs. The S,N-CQDs have been synthesized within 4 min via a one-pot, one-step, low-energy, and chemicals-free carbonization using a regular microwave. Full characterization of the S,N-CQDs has been conducted by TEM, Zeta potential measurement, fluorescence, UV-VIS, and IR spectroscopy. The prepared S,N-CQDs showed excellent water solubility and stability beside high fluorescence quantum yield of 15.2%. Thus, the prepared S,N-CQDs showed a superb potential as a pharmaceutical and biological sensor. The S,N-CQDs were applied as a fluorescence nanoprobe for determination of the widely used drug NTZ in pharmaceutical capsules over the concentration range of 0.25–50.0 μM with %recovery ±SD of 98.14 ± 0.42. Furthermore, the S,N-CQDs exhibited excellent performance as a biological sensor for determination of Hb at low concentrations with a minimum detectability of 10.30 nM and good precision. These results recommend the application of the designed nanoprobe for trace detection of Hb in conditions like hemolysis. The devised analytical techniques showed excellent greenness character being based on cheap, available, renewable, and safe plant biomass, exclude the use of hazardous organic solvents or harmful chemicals, consume little energy, time, and effort, adopt a one-step synthesis procedure, and mix-and-read analytical approach.
